title: CLOSE THE GAP: DIAGNOSTIC
intro:
CHAPTER 1 — CLOSE THE GAP: DIAGNOSTIC

Difficulty scale used in every chapter: 1 = warm-up, 3 = effortful, 5 = near-native pressure.

drills.title: Gap Hunt
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Pick one technical topic from your studies.
2. Record yourself speaking about it in English for 90 seconds.
3. Every time you can't produce a word you know in writing, say "gap" and keep talking.
4. Stop recording. Replay once.
5. Write down every gap word.
Rules:
- Do not stop the recording to search for a word.
- Do not pick a rehearsed topic.
How to check: You have 3+ gap words written down. If zero, redo with a harder topic.
drills.check: You have 3+ gap words written down. If zero, redo with a harder topic.

drills.title: Timed Answer
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Get 5 questions about your work or studies from another person or an AI voice tool.
2. Start speaking within 3 seconds of each question ending.
3. Record all 5 answers.
Rules:
- No rehearsing an answer while the question is being asked.
- No restarting an answer once started.
How to check: 5 recorded answers, each started within 3 seconds.
drills.check: 5 recorded answers, each started within 3 seconds.

drills.title: Honest Rewrite
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Take one answer from Drill 1.2.
2. Write it properly, as an email, with unlimited time.
3. Compare spoken transcript vs. written version side by side.
4. Write down what information the spoken version lost.
Rules:
- Do not score grammar. Score information lost only.
How to check: You can name one specific detail missing from the spoken version.
drills.check: You can name one specific detail missing from the spoken version.

mistakes: Buying a vocabulary list instead of doing Drill 1.1.
Studying more grammar rules before finishing this chapter.
Rehearsing answers silently before recording.
Skipping playback of your own recordings.
Choosing an easy topic so no gaps appear.
Judging Drill 1.3 by grammar instead of missing information.
Comparing your recordings to a native speaker instead of to your own next attempt.
mission: Have one unscripted English conversation, 5+ minutes, not about learning English. Record it if possible. Write 2 sentences after: one thing communicated successfully, one gap moment.
checklist: Drill 1.1 recorded, reviewed, gap words listed
Drill 1.2: 5 answers recorded, each started within 3 seconds
Drill 1.3 comparison written
Real-World Mission done, 2 sentences written
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: TRAIN RETRIEVAL, NOT RECOGNITION

drills.title: Recall vs. Recognition Test
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Take your Ch.1 Drill 1.1 gap word list.
2. Cover the list. Try to say each word out loud from memory, prompted only by the concept (not the word).
3. Mark each word: Recalled / Not Recalled.
4. Uncover the list. Read each missed word once, out loud, in a full sentence.
Rules:
- No looking at the list before attempting recall.
- No silent reading as a substitute for speaking the word.
How to check: You have a Recalled/Not Recalled count for every word on the list.
drills.check: You have a Recalled/Not Recalled count for every word on the list.

drills.title: 24-Hour Retrieval Gap
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Wait at least 24 hours after Drill 2.1.
2. Without looking at the list, try to recall and say all Ch.1 gap words again, out loud.
3. Record this attempt.
4. Compare to Drill 2.1's Recalled count.
Rules:
- Do not review the list in the 24 hours between drills.
How to check: A second Recalled/Not Recalled count exists, timestamped 24+ hours after the first.
drills.check: A second Recalled/Not Recalled count exists, timestamped 24+ hours after the first.

drills.title: Interleaved Retrieval Round
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Prepare 3 short task cards: (a) 5 vocabulary items from Ch.1, (b) 3 minimal pairs you can pronounce differently, (c) 2 grammar structures from memory (e.g., "describe something using present perfect").
2. Do one item from card (a), out loud.
3. Switch immediately to one item from card (b).
4. Switch immediately to one item from card (c).
5. Repeat the cycle until all items are done, always switching after one item.
Rules:
- Never do two items from the same card back to back.
- Every item spoken out loud, recorded.
How to check: Recording shows alternation across all three categories, no two consecutive same-category items.
drills.check: Recording shows alternation across all three categories, no two consecutive same-category items.

mistakes: Re-reading the gap list instead of attempting recall first.
Doing Drill 2.2 early, before 24 hours pass.
Blocking practice by category (all vocabulary, then all pronunciation, then all grammar) instead of interleaving.
Treating a high score on Drill 2.1 as mastery — recall right after exposure is expected to be easy and proves little.
Stopping retrieval attempts as soon as a word feels "on the tip of my tongue" instead of pushing to actually produce it.
mission: In a real conversation today, deliberately use at least 2 words from your Ch.1 gap list. Note afterward, in writing, whether they came out automatically or required a conscious search.
checklist: Drill 2.1 completed with Recalled/Not Recalled counts
Drill 2.2 completed 24+ hours later, second count logged
Drill 2.3 recorded, category alternation confirmed
Real-World Mission done, 2 gap words used, outcome noted
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: CONVERT PASSIVE VOCABULARY TO ACTIVE
intro: CHAPTER 3 — CONVERT PASSIVE VOCABULARY TO ACTIVE

drills.title: Passive Word Audit
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Open a technical text you understand well (textbook, paper, article).
2. Find 10 words or phrases you recognize instantly while reading but rarely say out loud.
3. Write them in your Active Vocabulary Bank (Appendix E) under today's date.
4. Try to say each one out loud, alone, right now, with no sentence around it.
Rules:
- Words must come from real material you were already reading, not a vocabulary list.
- Say each word out loud at least once before moving to Drill 3.2.
How to check: 10 words logged in the Active Vocabulary Bank.
drills.check: 10 words logged in the Active Vocabulary Bank.

drills.title: Sentence Forge
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Take the 10 words from Drill 3.1.
2. For each word, speak one original sentence out loud using it, about your own life or work.
3. Record all 10 sentences.
4. Replay. Mark each: Natural / Forced.
Rules:
- No copying example sentences from a dictionary or textbook.
- No writing the sentence first — speak it first, write it after if needed.
How to check: 10 recorded sentences, each marked Natural or Forced.
drills.check: 10 recorded sentences, each marked Natural or Forced.

drills.title: Speed Conversion Round
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Have someone read you the 10 words in random order (or record yourself reading them with 4-second gaps, then respond in the gaps).
2. For each word, say a full sentence using it within 3 seconds of hearing it.
3. Time each response.
4. Repeat the full round once more, same list, same method.
Rules:
- 3-second start rule, no exceptions.
- Second round must beat the first round's average time.
How to check: Two timed rounds logged, second round average time lower than first.
drills.check: Two timed rounds logged, second round average time lower than first.

drills.title: 24-Hour Bank Check
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Wait 24 hours.
2. Without looking, try to recall and speak all 10 words from this week's Active Vocabulary Bank entries.
3. Mark each: Active (recalled instantly) / Still Passive (not recalled).
4. Words marked Still Passive go back into Drill 3.2 tomorrow.
Rules:
- No reviewing the list before attempting recall.
How to check: All 10 words marked Active or Still Passive.
drills.check: All 10 words marked Active or Still Passive.

mistakes: Adding words to the Active Vocabulary Bank without ever speaking them.
Choosing rare or advanced words instead of common ones you actually need.
Writing sentences before speaking them.
Marking a word Active after one successful recall — recall must hold up after the 24-hour gap.
Letting the bank grow past 10 new words a week — more words than you can convert is not progress.
mission: In one real conversation, email, or presentation today, use 3 words from your Active Vocabulary Bank. Log which 3 and whether they came out automatically.
checklist: Drill 3.1: 10 words logged in Active Vocabulary Bank
Drill 3.2: 10 sentences recorded and marked Natural/Forced
Drill 3.3: two timed rounds completed, improvement confirmed
Drill 3.4 completed 24+ hours later, all words marked Active/Still Passive
Real-World Mission done, 3 words used and logged
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: RETRIEVAL-FIRST STUDY PROTOCOL

drills.title: Retrieval-First Listening
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Pick a 3-5 minute English audio or video clip you haven't heard before.
2. Listen once, no pausing, no notes.
3. Close the clip. Speak out loud, recorded, everything you remember — content and specific phrases.
4. Replay the clip once. Note what you missed.
5. Speak a second summary, recorded, including the missed material.
Rules:
- No note-taking during first listen.
- Recall attempt happens before replay, not after.
How to check: Two recorded summaries exist, second one longer or more accurate than the first.
drills.check: Two recorded summaries exist, second one longer or more accurate than the first.

drills.title: Retrieval-First Reading
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Pick a short technical passage (1-2 paragraphs) you haven't read before.
2. Read it once, normal pace.
3. Close the text. Speak out loud, recorded, a full explanation of what it said.
4. Reopen the text. Compare against what you said.
5. Speak a corrected version, recorded.
Rules:
- No rereading before the first recall attempt.
- Recall must be spoken, not just thought through.
How to check: Two recorded explanations exist, second one corrected against the source.
drills.check: Two recorded explanations exist, second one corrected against the source.

drills.title: Recognition Trap Test
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Take 5 words from your Active Vocabulary Bank (Ch.3).
2. Recognition test: read each word silently, ask yourself "do I know this?" Answer yes/no.
3. Recall test: cover the words. Say each one out loud from the concept alone, no word shown.
4. Compare: how many you were confident on in step 2 vs. how many you actually produced in step 3.
Rules:
- Step 2 and step 3 must be done separately, not combined.
How to check: A written gap between recognition-confidence count and recall-success count.
drills.check: A written gap between recognition-confidence count and recall-success count.

drills.title: Replace One Passive Habit
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Name one study habit you currently use that involves rereading or relistening without recall (reviewing notes, replaying a lecture recording, rereading vocabulary lists).
2. Do that activity once, exactly as you normally would, but stop before any rereading/relistening pass.
3. At the stopping point, speak out loud everything you can recall, recorded.
4. Only then, complete the reread/relisten pass.
Rules:
- The recall attempt must come before the second pass, permanently, starting today.
How to check: One habit identified and converted, with a recorded recall attempt.
drills.check: One habit identified and converted, with a recorded recall attempt.

mistakes: Rereading or relistening before attempting recall.
Testing recall immediately after exposure and calling it mastery — recall must survive a delay to count (see Ch.2 Drill 2.2).
Treating "I recognize this" as equivalent to "I can produce this."
Giving up on a recall attempt after a few seconds instead of pushing to finish it.
Taking notes during first exposure instead of during the recall attempt.
mission: After today's lecture, meeting, or video you watch for real reasons: before checking your notes or rewatching anything, speak out loud, recorded, everything you remember. Then check notes and note what you missed.
checklist: Drill 4.1: two recorded listening summaries
Drill 4.2: two recorded reading explanations
Drill 4.3: recognition vs. recall gap documented
Drill 4.4: one passive habit converted, recall attempt recorded
Real-World Mission done, recall recorded before notes checked
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: THINK IN ENGLISH, NOT THROUGH IT
intro: CHAPTER 5 — THINK IN ENGLISH, NOT THROUGH IT

drills.title: Translation Detection
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Record yourself narrating your surroundings out loud in English for 60 seconds — what you see, what you're doing.
2. Replay. Mark every pause longer than 1 second with a timestamp.
3. For each marked pause, ask: was a native-language sentence forming before the English came out?
4. Write yes/no for each marked pause.
Rules:
- Narrate live, no planning before recording starts.
How to check: A timestamped list of pauses, each marked yes or no.
drills.check: A timestamped list of pauses, each marked yes or no.

drills.title: Direct Description Sprint
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Look around you. Point at or look at 15 objects, one at a time.
2. For each, say one English sentence about it within 2 seconds of looking at it.
3. Record the full sprint.
4. Replay. Mark any sentence where the pause before speaking was longer than 2 seconds.
Rules:
- 2-second start rule, no exceptions.
- No planning the sentence before looking at the object.
How to check: 15 sentences recorded, marked count of over-2-second pauses.

DRILL 5.3 — Inner English Log                    Difficulty: 2/5   Time: ongoing, 5 min to log
Steps:
1. Today, during 3 separate ordinary moments (walking, waiting, eating), deliberately form one full thought in English silently, then say it out loud immediately after forming it.
2. Log each of the 3 instances: the thought, and whether it formed directly in English or was translated first.
Rules:
- The spoken-out-loud step is mandatory, not optional — silent thought alone does not count.
How to check: 3 logged instances with direct/translated marked for each.
drills.check: 15 sentences recorded, marked count of over-2-second pauses.

drills.title: L1 Interference Catch
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Replay any recording from Ch.1-4.
2. Listen for word order, missing articles, or sentence structure that matches your native language pattern instead of English pattern.
3. Mark 3 specific instances.
4. For each, say the corrected English version out loud, recorded.
Rules:
- Instances must be specific and timestamped, not general impressions.
How to check: 3 corrected sentences recorded.
drills.check: 3 corrected sentences recorded.

mistakes: Giving yourself time to translate before speaking in any drill.
Doing Drill 5.3 silently and skipping the spoken-out-loud step.
Treating one translation-free recording as proof the habit is gone.
Ignoring short pauses under 1 second — those are normal and not translation.
Correcting L1 interference errors silently instead of speaking the fix out loud.
mission: Pick one everyday task today (cooking, commuting, cleaning). Narrate your thoughts out loud in English in real time for at least 10 minutes while doing it. Note afterward how many times you noticed translating first.
checklist: Drill 5.1: pause list with yes/no translation marks
Drill 5.2: 15 sentences recorded, over-2-second pauses counted
Drill 5.3: 3 instances logged
Drill 5.4: 3 L1 interference instances caught and corrected aloud
Real-World Mission done, 10+ minutes narrated, translation count noted
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: FIX YOUR SPECIFIC SOUNDS

drills.title: Sound Inventory Check
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Replay your Part 0, Section 2 word-pair recording (ship/sheep, think/sink, very/berry, light/right, world/word, thirty/dirty, vowel/bowel, worked/walked, live/leave, cheap/chip).
2. For each pair, mark: Distinct / Collapsed (sounded the same).
3. List your Collapsed pairs. These are your target sounds for this chapter.
Rules:
- Use the Part 0 recording, not a new one — you're checking existing evidence.
How to check: A written list of target sound pairs, at least 1, at most 6.
drills.check: A written list of target sound pairs, at least 1, at most 6.

drills.title: Isolated Sound Repetition
drills.body:
Steps:
1. For each target pair from Drill 6.1, say each of the two words alone, slowly, 5 times each, recorded.
2. Replay. For each repetition, mark: Distinct / Still Collapsed.
3. If still collapsed after 5 reps, search "[sound] vs [sound] mouth position" for a 1-minute tutorial, watch once, redo the 5 reps.
Rules:
- All repetitions recorded, not silent practice.
How to check: 5 marked repetitions per word, per target pair.
drills.check: 5 marked repetitions per word, per target pair.

drills.title: Minimal Pair Ping-Pong
drills.body:
Steps:
1. For each target pair, alternate the two words rapidly 10 times: word A, word B, word A, word B...
2. Record the full sequence.
3. Have another person (or replay it yourself after a break) identify which word they hear for each repetition, without seeing the list.
4. Score: correct identifications out of 10 per pair.
Rules:
- Alternation must be rapid, not slow and deliberate.
How to check: A correct-identification score per target pair.
drills.check: A correct-identification score per target pair.

drills.title: Sound Transfer to New Words
drills.body:
Steps:
1. For each target sound, find 3 new words (not from the original list) containing that sound.
2. Say each new word in a full sentence, recorded.
3. Replay and self-mark: Distinct / Collapsed.
Rules:
- New words must come from material you actually use (your field, your daily vocabulary).
How to check: 3 new words per target sound, each recorded in a sentence.
drills.check: 3 new words per target sound, each recorded in a sentence.

mistakes: Practicing sounds that were already Distinct in Drill 6.1 — target only the Collapsed ones.
Skipping playback and self-marking — you can't fix what you don't hear.
Practicing silently instead of recording every repetition.
Stopping after Drill 6.2 without testing transfer to new words in Drill 6.4.
Expecting full correction in one session — return to Collapsed pairs again next week.
mission: In one real conversation today, deliberately use 3 words containing your target sounds. Note whether the listener asked you to repeat anything.
checklist: Drill 6.1: target sound pairs identified from Part 0 data
Drill 6.2: 5 reps per word per pair, recorded and marked
Drill 6.3: ping-pong score logged per pair
Drill 6.4: 3 new words per sound, recorded in sentences
Real-World Mission done, listener reaction noted
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: PRIORITIZE FOR INTELLIGIBILITY

drills.title: Intelligibility Impact Ranking
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Record yourself reading a new 100-word technical paragraph aloud, normal speed.
2. Run the recording through a speech-to-text tool (phone dictation or transcription app).
3. Compare the transcript to what you actually said.
4. List every word the tool got wrong. These are your highest-impact error patterns.
Rules:
- Use a transcription tool, not your own ear, for this drill — it catches what self-review misses.
How to check: A list of mistranscribed words with the error pattern named (e.g., "final -s dropped," "stress on wrong syllable").
drills.check: A list of mistranscribed words with the error pattern named (e.g., "final -s dropped," "stress on wrong syllable").

drills.title: Top 3 Pattern Drill
drills.body:
Steps:
1. From Drill 7.1, pick your 3 most frequent error patterns.
2. For each pattern, find or write 5 words that contain it.
3. Say each word 3 times, recorded, deliberately exaggerating the correct version.
4. Say each word once at normal conversational speed, recorded.
Rules:
- Only work the 3 patterns identified — do not add others.
How to check: 5 words x 3 patterns, each recorded exaggerated and at normal speed.
drills.check: 5 words x 3 patterns, each recorded exaggerated and at normal speed.

drills.title: Listener Comprehension Test
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Read a new paragraph aloud to a real person (in person, call, or voice message).
2. Ask them to repeat back or write down what they heard, without you helping.
3. Compare their version to what you actually said.
4. Log every mismatch.
Rules:
- Do not correct or repeat yourself during their listening attempt.
How to check: A logged comparison with at least one real listener, mismatches identified.
drills.check: A logged comparison with at least one real listener, mismatches identified.

drills.title: Final Sound and Cluster Drill
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Pick 8 words ending in consonant clusters or single final consonants common in your speech (e.g., "asked," "months," "world," "worked").
2. Say each word alone 3 times, recorded, making sure the final sound is audible.
3. Say each word at the end of a full sentence, recorded.
4. Replay and mark: Final sound audible / Final sound dropped.
Rules:
- Focus only on the final sound, not the whole word.
How to check: 8 words marked audible/dropped in both isolated and sentence-final position.
drills.check: 8 words marked audible/dropped in both isolated and sentence-final position.

mistakes: Trying to fix every accent feature at once instead of the top 3 from Drill 7.1.
Relying only on your own ear instead of a transcription tool or real listener.
Treating this chapter as accent elimination — the target is being understood, not sounding native.
Skipping Drill 7.3 because it feels uncomfortable asking someone to repeat what you said.
Fixing final sounds in isolation but never testing them inside full sentences.
mission: Ask one real person today, directly: "Was there anything I said that was hard to understand?" Log their answer exactly, even if it's short or vague.
checklist: Drill 7.1: transcription comparison done, error patterns listed
Drill 7.2: top 3 patterns drilled, 5 words each
Drill 7.3: real listener comprehension test logged
Drill 7.4: 8 words checked for final-sound clarity
Real-World Mission done, listener feedback logged
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: HIT THE BEAT, NOT EVERY SYLLABLE
intro: CHAPTER 8 — HIT THE BEAT, NOT EVERY SYLLABLE

drills.title: Beat Tapping
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Take this sentence: "The ENgineer CHECKED the REsults beFORE the MEETing."
2. Tap your hand on each capitalized (stressed) syllable while saying the sentence aloud.
3. Keep the gap between taps roughly equal, even though the number of syllables between taps varies.
4. Record yourself doing this 5 times.
Rules:
- Taps must land on stressed syllables only, evenly spaced in time.
How to check: 5 recordings where tap spacing sounds roughly even, regardless of syllable count between taps.
drills.check: 5 recordings where tap spacing sounds roughly even, regardless of syllable count between taps.

drills.title: Compression Drill
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Take 5 sentences from your own recent recordings (Ch.1-7).
2. Mark the 2-3 stressed (content) words in each sentence.
3. Say each sentence aloud, compressing the unstressed words between stresses so the stressed words land at even intervals.
4. Record each attempt.
Rules:
- Do not drop unstressed words entirely — compress them, don't delete them.
How to check: 5 recorded sentences with identifiable even stress intervals.
drills.check: 5 recorded sentences with identifiable even stress intervals.

drills.title: Rhythm Shadowing
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Pick a 30-second native-speaker audio clip (interview, podcast).
2. Play it. Speak along simultaneously, matching timing and stress placement, not exact words if you fall behind.
3. Record your shadowing attempt.
4. Repeat 3 times, aiming to match the rhythm more closely each time.
Rules:
- Priority is matching rhythm/stress timing, not perfect word accuracy.
How to check: 3 recorded shadowing attempts of the same clip.
drills.check: 3 recorded shadowing attempts of the same clip.

drills.title: Side-by-Side Comparison
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Play your best Drill 8.3 attempt next to the original clip, one sentence at a time.
2. For each sentence, mark: Rhythm Matched / Rhythm Off.
3. For sentences marked Off, say that sentence 3 more times, recorded, targeting the stressed beats only.
Rules:
- Compare rhythm and timing only, not accent or exact pronunciation.
How to check: Every sentence marked, and Off sentences redrilled.
drills.check: Every sentence marked, and Off sentences redrilled.

mistakes: Giving equal time and stress to every syllable, instead of compressing unstressed ones.
Dropping unstressed words completely instead of compressing them.
Shadowing for word accuracy instead of rhythm accuracy in Drill 8.3.
Skipping the tapping step — the physical beat is what trains the timing, not just listening.
Practicing rhythm only on isolated sentences and never on your own natural speech.
mission: In one real sentence during a conversation today, deliberately compress the unstressed words and hit the 2-3 stressed words evenly. Note afterward whether it felt natural or forced.
checklist: Drill 8.1: 5 tapped recordings completed
Drill 8.2: 5 sentences compressed and recorded
Drill 8.3: 3 shadowing attempts recorded
Drill 8.4: comparison done, Off sentences redrilled
Real-World Mission done, outcome noted
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: STRESS WHAT MATTERS, DROP WHAT DOESN'T
intro: CHAPTER 9 — STRESS WHAT MATTERS, DROP WHAT DOESN'T

drills.title: Content vs. Function Tagging
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Take 5 sentences from your Ch.1-8 recordings.
2. Write each sentence down. Underline the content words (nouns, main verbs, adjectives, adverbs, negatives).
3. Leave function words unmarked (articles, prepositions, auxiliary verbs, pronouns, conjunctions).
4. Say each sentence aloud, stressing only the underlined words, recorded.
Rules:
- Only content words get stress — function words stay short and unstressed.
How to check: 5 sentences tagged and recorded with correct stress placement.
drills.check: 5 sentences tagged and recorded with correct stress placement.

drills.title: Meaning-Shift Drill
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Take this sentence: "I didn't say she took the report."
2. Say it 7 times, each time stressing a different single word (I / didn't / say / she / took / the / report).
3. Record all 7 versions.
4. After each, state out loud in one sentence what that stress implies.
Rules:
- Only one word stressed per repetition.
How to check: 7 recorded versions, each with a stated meaning difference.
drills.check: 7 recorded versions, each with a stated meaning difference.

drills.title: Function Word Reduction
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Take 5 sentences containing "to," "for," "of," "and," "can," or "was."
2. Say each sentence at normal conversational speed, reducing these function words (to → "tuh," and → "n," can → "kn").
3. Record each.
4. Replay and mark: Reduced naturally / Still full form.
Rules:
- Reduction applies only to function words, never content words.
How to check: 5 sentences recorded and marked.
drills.check: 5 sentences recorded and marked.

drills.title: Cold Sentence Stress Test
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Have someone give you 5 new sentences you haven't seen (or generate them from an AI tool).
2. Say each aloud immediately, applying correct content-word stress without preparation time.
3. Record all 5.
4. Self-mark: Correct stress / Incorrect stress.
Rules:
- No advance preparation — sentences must be new to you.
How to check: 5 recorded sentences, each marked.
drills.check: 5 recorded sentences, each marked.

mistakes: Stressing function words out of habit from syllable-timed native speech.
Stressing every word equally instead of picking the content words.
Over-reducing function words to the point they disappear entirely.
Practicing only prepared sentences and skipping the cold test in Drill 9.4.
Forgetting that stress placement can change meaning (Drill 9.2) — treating stress as decoration instead of information.
mission: In one real conversation today, deliberately stress only the content words in 3 sentences you say. Note whether it changed how confident or clear you sounded.
checklist: Drill 9.1: 5 sentences tagged and recorded
Drill 9.2: 7 stress-shift versions recorded with meaning stated
Drill 9.3: 5 sentences recorded with function-word reduction
Drill 9.4: 5 cold sentences recorded and self-marked
Real-World Mission done, outcome noted
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: CONTROL YOUR PITCH

drills.title: Statement vs. Question Melody
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Take the phrase "You finished the report."
2. Say it as a statement: pitch falls at the end. Record.
3. Say it as a yes/no question: pitch rises at the end. Record.
4. Repeat with 4 more phrases from your own recent work.
Rules:
- The only difference between the two versions must be pitch direction at the end, not word order.
How to check: 10 recordings (5 phrases x 2 versions), rise/fall clearly audible on replay.
drills.check: 10 recordings (5 phrases x 2 versions), rise/fall clearly audible on replay.

drills.title: Certainty vs. Uncertainty Pitch
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Take 3 opinion sentences about your work ("I think this approach is correct.").
2. Say each with confident falling pitch at the end. Record.
3. Say each with uncertain rising pitch at the end. Record.
4. Replay both versions back to back for each sentence.
Rules:
- Wording stays identical between the two versions — only pitch changes.
How to check: 6 recordings, confident and uncertain versions clearly distinct.
drills.check: 6 recordings, confident and uncertain versions clearly distinct.

drills.title: Wh-Question vs. Yes/No Question
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Take 4 wh-questions ("What did you find?") and say each with falling pitch at the end. Record.
2. Take 4 yes/no questions ("Did you find it?") and say each with rising pitch at the end. Record.
3. Replay all 8 and self-mark: Correct pattern / Incorrect pattern.
Rules:
- Wh-questions fall, yes/no questions rise — no exceptions in this drill.
How to check: 8 recordings, each self-marked.
drills.check: 8 recordings, each self-marked.

drills.title: List and Signal Intonation
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Take a 4-item list from your field ("We tested speed, accuracy, cost, and reliability.").
2. Say it with rising pitch on each non-final item and falling pitch only on the last item. Record.
3. Take one sentence that signals you're not finished speaking ("So, given that data—") and say it with pitch that stays open/unfinished. Record.
4. Take one sentence that signals completion ("—and that's why we chose this approach.") with clear falling pitch. Record.
Rules:
- Final item in a list always falls; all prior items rise slightly.
How to check: 3 recordings with the correct pitch pattern for list, continuation, and completion.
drills.check: 3 recordings with the correct pitch pattern for list, continuation, and completion.

mistakes: Using rising pitch on every sentence, including statements, making everything sound like a question.
Speaking in a flat, unchanging pitch with no rise or fall at all.
Confusing wh-question and yes/no question patterns.
Using uncertain rising pitch when stating a confident opinion in real conversation.
Practicing intonation only in isolated drills and never checking it in the Real-World Mission.
mission: In one real meeting or conversation today, state one opinion using confident falling pitch, deliberately. Note afterward how it felt and whether it changed how the response landed.
checklist: Drill 10.1: 10 recordings, statement/question contrast clear
Drill 10.2: 6 recordings, confident/uncertain contrast clear
Drill 10.3: 8 recordings, self-marked correct/incorrect
Drill 10.4: 3 recordings covering list, continuation, completion
Real-World Mission done, outcome noted
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: LINK YOUR WORDS TOGETHER

drills.title: Linking Detection
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Pick a 30-second native-speaker audio clip.
2. Find the written transcript (captions or a transcription tool).
3. Mark every place a consonant sound connects into the next word's vowel sound (e.g., "turn it off" sounds like "tur-ni-toff").
4. Say each linked phrase aloud, matching what you heard, recorded.
Rules:
- Mark at least 5 linking points in the clip.
How to check: 5 marked linking points, each reproduced aloud and recorded.
drills.check: 5 marked linking points, each reproduced aloud and recorded.

drills.title: Linking Production
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Take 5 phrases from your own recordings (Ch.1-10) where a word ends in a consonant and the next starts with a vowel ("turn it off," "check it out," "an idea").
2. Say each phrase twice: once fully separated, once fully linked.
3. Record both versions of each.
Rules:
- The linked version must connect the sounds smoothly, no pause between words.
How to check: 10 recordings (5 phrases x 2 versions), linked version audibly smoother.
drills.check: 10 recordings (5 phrases x 2 versions), linked version audibly smoother.

drills.title: Elision Drill
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Take these phrases: "next day," "must be," "didn't know," "what do you," "kind of."
2. Say each at normal conversational speed, allowing the natural dropped sound (next day → "nex day," what do you → "whaddaya").
3. Record each.
4. Say each again in a full sentence of your own, recorded.
Rules:
- Elision applies at normal speed only — do not force it artificially slow.
How to check: 5 phrases recorded isolated and in a full sentence.
drills.check: 5 phrases recorded isolated and in a full sentence.

drills.title: Connected Speech Dictation
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Pick a new 20-second native-speaker clip with natural, fast speech.
2. Listen and write down exactly what you hear, including where words seem to merge.
3. Check against the real transcript. Mark what you missed.
4. Shadow the clip 3 times, recorded, matching the linking and elision you now know is there.
Rules:
- Write your first-pass transcript before checking the real one.
How to check: A first-pass transcript, a comparison, and 3 recorded shadowing attempts.
drills.check: A first-pass transcript, a comparison, and 3 recorded shadowing attempts.

mistakes: Over-enunciating every word separately, which sounds robotic and unnatural.
Treating linking and elision as sloppy speech instead of standard native pattern.
Forcing elision into slow, careful speech where it doesn't naturally occur.
Skipping Drill 11.4's first-pass transcript and going straight to the answer.
Practicing linking only on prepared phrases and never testing it on new material.
mission: In one real conversation today, deliberately use 3 linked phrases at natural speed. Note whether it felt more natural than speaking word-by-word.
checklist: Drill 11.1: 5 linking points marked and reproduced
Drill 11.2: 10 recordings, separated vs. linked
Drill 11.3: 5 phrases recorded isolated and in sentences
Drill 11.4: transcript, comparison, 3 shadowing attempts
Real-World Mission done, outcome noted
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: THE FULL SHADOWING PROTOCOL

drills.title: Material Selection
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Find 3 candidate clips (30-90 seconds each): one interview, one podcast segment, one lecture or talk.
2. For each, check: clear audio, natural speed (not slowed), transcript or captions available, content you can mostly follow already.
3. Reject any clip you understand less than 70% of on first listen.
4. Pick 1 clip that passes all checks. This is your material for Drills 12.2-12.4.
Rules:
- Do not pick material based on interest alone — it must pass the comprehension check.
How to check: 1 selected clip meeting all 4 criteria.
drills.check: 1 selected clip meeting all 4 criteria.

drills.title: Level 1: Transcript-Assisted Shadowing
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Read the transcript once, silently, to confirm you understand it.
2. Play the clip. Read along and speak simultaneously, matching timing, rhythm, and stress.
3. Record your shadowing.
4. Repeat 3 times.
Rules:
- Speak at full volume, not muttering.
- Simultaneous means speaking while the audio plays, not after it stops.
How to check: 3 recorded attempts with transcript support.
drills.check: 3 recorded attempts with transcript support.

drills.title: Level 2: Blind Shadowing
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Same clip. Remove the transcript.
2. Play the clip. Shadow simultaneously, from memory and listening alone.
3. Record 3 attempts.
4. After each, mark any section where you fell silent or fell behind.
Rules:
- No transcript visible at any point during this drill.
How to check: 3 recorded attempts, weak sections marked.
drills.check: 3 recorded attempts, weak sections marked.

drills.title: Level 3: Delayed Shadowing
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Same clip. Play it.
2. Start speaking about 2 seconds after the audio starts, repeating what you just heard while continuing to listen to what comes next.
3. Record 3 attempts.
4. Mark sections where the delay caused you to lose track.
Rules:
- The 2-second lag must be maintained throughout, not closed to zero.
How to check: 3 recorded attempts with a consistent lag maintained.

DRILL 12.5 — Mastery Loop                          Difficulty: 3/5   Time: 10 min/day
Steps:
1. Return to the same clip daily for up to 5 days.
2. Each day, do one Level 2 (blind) attempt, recorded.
3. Compare each day's recording to the previous day's.
4. Stop the loop and select a new clip once 3 consecutive days show no audible improvement, or once the shadowing sounds near-identical to the original in timing and stress.
Rules:
- Do not switch clips before at least 3 days on the same one.
How to check: A dated recording for each day of the loop, with a stop point marked.
drills.check: 3 recorded attempts with a consistent lag maintained.

mistakes: Jumping straight to blind or delayed shadowing before completing Level 1.
Choosing material below 70% comprehension, making shadowing a guessing exercise instead of a production drill.
Muttering quietly instead of speaking at full volume.
Switching clips every session instead of running the Mastery Loop on one clip.
Shadowing for word-perfect accuracy only and ignoring rhythm and stress, which matter more here (see Ch.8, Ch.9).
mission: Take any video or audio you encounter naturally today (not chosen for practice). Shadow a 30-second segment of it once, blind, recorded. Note how it compares to shadowing prepared material.
checklist: Drill 12.1: 1 clip selected, criteria confirmed
Drill 12.2: 3 Level 1 attempts recorded
Drill 12.3: 3 Level 2 attempts recorded, weak sections marked
Drill 12.4: 3 Level 3 attempts recorded, lag maintained
Drill 12.5: Mastery Loop run for 3-5 days, stop point marked
Real-World Mission done, comparison noted
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: BUILD YOUR OWN FEEDBACK LOOP
intro: CHAPTER 13 — BUILD YOUR OWN FEEDBACK LOOP

drills.title: Build Your Self-Analysis Rubric
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Create a table with 5 rows: Fluency (pausing/flow), Pronunciation (target sounds from Ch.6-7), Rhythm/Stress (Ch.8-9), Intonation (Ch.10), Connected Speech (Ch.11).
2. For each row, write a 0-4 scale with one concrete marker per score (e.g., Fluency 0 = "breaks down before 15 seconds," 4 = "continuous, native-like pausing").
3. Save this rubric — it is reused in every future chapter's self-analysis.
Rules:
- Each score level must have a specific, checkable description, not a vague label.
How to check: A complete 5-row, 0-4 rubric saved in Appendix E.
drills.check: A complete 5-row, 0-4 rubric saved in Appendix E.

drills.title: Score a Fresh Recording
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Record yourself speaking on any topic for 90 seconds, unrehearsed.
2. Replay once for content, once per rubric row (5 replays total, one focus each).
3. Score all 5 rows using Drill 13.1's rubric.
4. Write one specific piece of evidence per score (a timestamp or example, not just a number).
Rules:
- Each score needs written evidence — a number alone does not count.
How to check: 5 scores, each with one piece of written evidence.
drills.check: 5 scores, each with one piece of written evidence.

drills.title: Before/After Comparison
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Pull up your Part 0 Speaking Fluency recording (Section 1) or Ch.1 Drill 1.2 recording.
2. Score that old recording using the Drill 13.1 rubric.
3. Record a new 90-second answer to the same or equivalent prompt.
4. Score the new recording using the same rubric.
5. Write the score difference per row.
Rules:
- Use the identical rubric for both recordings — no adjusting criteria between them.
How to check: Two full rubric scorings and a written difference per row.
drills.check: Two full rubric scorings and a written difference per row.

drills.title: Set the Weekly Habit
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Pick one fixed day each week for self-analysis going forward.
2. Write this day into your practice log as a recurring entry.
3. Record one 90-second sample now as the first entry in this weekly cycle.
Rules:
- The day must be fixed and recurring, not "whenever I feel like it."
How to check: A recurring day logged, one dated sample recording attached.
drills.check: A recurring day logged, one dated sample recording attached.

mistakes: Scoring a recording after listening only once, straight through.
Giving a score with no specific evidence attached.
Changing the rubric's criteria between recordings, making comparisons meaningless.
Avoiding listening to your own voice instead of using it as data.
Setting up the weekly habit and then skipping it after one week.
mission: Take today's Real-World Mission recording (from this chapter or an earlier one) and score it with the Drill 13.1 rubric. Log the scores.
checklist: Drill 13.1: 5-row rubric built and saved
Drill 13.2: fresh recording scored with evidence
Drill 13.3: before/after comparison completed with score differences
Drill 13.4: recurring weekly day set, first sample recorded
Real-World Mission scored using the rubric
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: LISTEN ACTIVELY, NOT PASSIVELY

drills.title: Prediction Drill
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Pick a 2-minute audio or video clip, new to you.
2. Play 10 seconds. Pause. Say out loud, recorded, what you think the next sentence will be about.
3. Play the next 10 seconds. Compare to your prediction.
4. Repeat this pause-predict-check cycle for the full clip.
Rules:
- Prediction must be spoken out loud before checking, every time.
How to check: A recording containing at least 8 prediction-check cycles.
drills.check: A recording containing at least 8 prediction-check cycles.

drills.title: Subtitle Weaning
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Watch a 3-minute clip with English subtitles on. Note your comprehension (rough %).
2. Watch the same clip again immediately, subtitles off. Note comprehension again.
3. Watch a different, new 3-minute clip with subtitles off from the start. Note comprehension.
4. Compare all three comprehension estimates.
Rules:
- The subtitles-off rewatch must happen immediately after the subtitled version, same clip.
How to check: Three logged comprehension estimates with the comparison written out.
drills.check: Three logged comprehension estimates with the comparison written out.

drills.title: Gap-Category Listening Log
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Listen to a 3-minute clip, no subtitles, no pausing.
2. Speak a recorded summary of what you understood.
3. Relisten once. For everything you missed, categorize it: Unknown Vocabulary / Too Fast / Unfamiliar Accent / Unclear Grammar Structure.
4. Log the category counts.
Rules:
- Every missed section must be assigned exactly one category.
How to check: A category count log (e.g., "6 vocabulary, 2 speed, 1 accent").
drills.check: A category count log (e.g., "6 vocabulary, 2 speed, 1 accent").

drills.title: Full Dictation Check
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Pick a new 20-second clip.
2. Transcribe it word-for-word by ear, replaying as needed, up to 5 times.
3. Check against the real transcript.
4. For every error, mark which Drill 14.3 category it belongs to.
Rules:
- Maximum 5 replays — if not complete by then, submit what you have.
How to check: A completed transcript attempt, compared and categorized against the source.
drills.check: A completed transcript attempt, compared and categorized against the source.

mistakes: Watching with subtitles as your default and never testing without them.
Predicting silently instead of speaking the prediction out loud.
Logging "I didn't understand" without identifying which category it falls into.
Replaying a dictation clip more than 5 times, turning the drill into an exercise in patience rather than listening.
Choosing only clear, slow content and avoiding natural-speed material.
mission: Watch or listen to one piece of media today that you'd normally put subtitles on. Do it without subtitles first. Log your comprehension estimate before checking subtitles or a transcript.
checklist: Drill 14.1: 8+ prediction-check cycles recorded
Drill 14.2: three comprehension estimates logged and compared
Drill 14.3: gap-category counts logged
Drill 14.4: dictation attempt completed and categorized
Real-World Mission done, comprehension estimate logged
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: COMPREHEND AT FULL NATIVE SPEED
intro: CHAPTER 15 — COMPREHEND AT FULL NATIVE SPEED

drills.title: Category-Targeted Attack
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Pull your Ch.14 Drill 14.3 gap-category log. Identify your highest-count category (Vocabulary / Speed / Accent / Grammar).
2. Find material matching that specific gap (e.g., if Speed is highest, find fast conversational clips; if Accent, find a specific regional accent).
3. Listen to 3 minutes of it, no subtitles.
4. Speak a recorded summary. Relisten once. Log new comprehension %.
Rules:
- Material must match the identified weak category, not be chosen randomly.
How to check: A before/after comprehension % for the targeted category.
drills.check: A before/after comprehension % for the targeted category.

drills.title: Speed Ramp
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Pick a 1-minute clip with adjustable playback speed.
2. Listen at 1.0x. Speak a recorded summary.
3. Listen at 1.25x. Speak a recorded summary.
4. Listen at 1.5x. Speak a recorded summary.
5. Compare summary accuracy across the three speeds.
Rules:
- Each speed gets exactly one listen before summarizing.
How to check: Three recorded summaries, one per speed, with accuracy compared.
drills.check: Three recorded summaries, one per speed, with accuracy compared.

drills.title: Bottom-Up Sound Chase
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Find 5 short fast phrases (3-5 words) from natural-speed native audio.
2. Play each phrase 3 times maximum.
3. Write exactly what you heard, sound by sound, even if it doesn't form real words yet.
4. Check against the actual phrase. Say the correct version aloud, recorded.
Rules:
- Maximum 3 replays per phrase.
How to check: 5 phrases attempted, checked, and corrected aloud.
drills.check: 5 phrases attempted, checked, and corrected aloud.

drills.title: Meeting-Speed Simulation
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Find a fast, unscripted native discussion (panel, debate, podcast conversation) at least 5 minutes long.
2. Listen straight through, no pausing, no subtitles.
3. Immediately after, answer out loud, recorded: What was the main disagreement or decision? What were 2 supporting points made?
4. Relisten once to check your answers.
Rules:
- No pausing during the first listen, regardless of comprehension gaps.
How to check: Recorded answers to both questions, checked against a relisten.
drills.check: Recorded answers to both questions, checked against a relisten.

mistakes: Staying permanently at slowed-down playback speed instead of progressing through the Speed Ramp.
Avoiding your weakest category from Ch.14 and practicing only what's already easy.
Replaying Drill 15.3 phrases more than 3 times, turning decoding into guessing from repetition.
Pausing during Drill 15.4's first listen, which defeats the meeting-speed simulation.
Treating one successful fast-speed session as proof the skill is built — this needs repeated exposure.
mission: Listen to or join one fast native conversation today for at least 5 minutes (a live conversation, call, or unscripted video). Immediately after, speak a recorded summary of the main points.
checklist: Drill 15.1: weak category targeted, before/after % logged
Drill 15.2: three speed levels recorded and compared
Drill 15.3: 5 phrases decoded and corrected
Drill 15.4: main point and 2 supporting points recorded and checked
Real-World Mission done, summary recorded
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: RUN A SPACED VOCABULARY PIPELINE
intro: CHAPTER 16 — RUN A SPACED VOCABULARY PIPELINE

drills.title: Build the Review Schedule
drills.body:
Steps:
1. In Appendix E, create a Vocabulary Pipeline table with columns: Word, Date Added, Day 3 Check, Day 7 Check, Day 14 Check, Day 30 Check, Graduated (Y/N).
2. This table extends your Active Vocabulary Bank from Ch.3 — do not create a separate system.
3. Set a recurring daily 5-minute slot to check which words are due for review today.
Rules:
- Every new word from now on goes into this table, not a separate list.
How to check: Table built, daily review slot scheduled.
drills.check: Table built, daily review slot scheduled.

drills.title: Weekly Intake Batch
drills.body:
Steps:
1. From this week's reading, lectures, or listening, collect 10 new words or chunks you needed but didn't have.
2. Add all 10 to the Vocabulary Pipeline table with today's date.
3. For each, speak one original sentence aloud, recorded.
Rules:
- Maximum 10 new words per week — do not exceed this intake limit.
How to check: 10 words logged with today's date, 10 sentences recorded.
drills.check: 10 words logged with today's date, 10 sentences recorded.

drills.title: Scheduled Review Session
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Check the Vocabulary Pipeline table for any word due today (Day 3, 7, 14, or 30 since it was added).
2. For each due word, attempt spoken recall from the concept alone, no looking at the word first.
3. Mark Recalled / Not Recalled in the matching column.
4. Words Not Recalled restart at Day 3 from today.
Rules:
- Recall attempt happens before looking at the word, every time.
How to check: Every due word marked for today's checkpoint.
drills.check: Every due word marked for today's checkpoint.

drills.title: Graduation Check
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Any word marked Recalled at all four checkpoints (Day 3, 7, 14, 30) is eligible for graduation.
2. For each eligible word, do one final cold test: use it correctly in a spoken sentence with no preparation, recorded.
3. If successful, mark Graduated: Y. Remove it from daily review rotation.
4. Graduated words free space for new intake in Drill 16.2.
Rules:
- All four checkpoints must show Recalled before graduation is possible.
How to check: Graduated column updated, cold-test recordings for each newly graduated word.
drills.check: Graduated column updated, cold-test recordings for each newly graduated word.

mistakes: Adding more than 10 new words per week, overloading the review schedule.
Looking at the word before attempting recall during a scheduled review.
Skipping review days and batching them later — spacing only works on schedule.
Marking a word Graduated without the final cold-test recording.
Keeping graduated words in the daily rotation instead of retiring them.
mission: Use one word that is due for review today in a real conversation or piece of writing. Log whether it came out automatically.
checklist: Drill 16.1: pipeline table built, daily slot scheduled
Drill 16.2: 10 new words logged, 10 sentences recorded
Drill 16.3: all due words checked for today
Drill 16.4: eligible words cold-tested and graduated
Real-World Mission done, outcome logged
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: SPEAK IN WORD PARTNERSHIPS

drills.title: Collocation Extraction
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Open a technical text or article you understand well.
2. Find 8 verb+noun or adjective+noun pairs that go together as fixed partnerships ("make a decision," "raise a concern," "conduct an experiment," "significant impact").
3. Add all 8 to your Vocabulary Pipeline (Ch.16) as single chunk entries, not separate words.
4. Say each one aloud in an original sentence, recorded.
Rules:
- Extract from real material, not a pre-made collocation list.
How to check: 8 chunks logged in the pipeline, 8 sentences recorded.
drills.check: 8 chunks logged in the pipeline, 8 sentences recorded.

drills.title: Error Correction Drill
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Say these 5 phrases aloud as you'd naturally produce them, recorded, before checking: "do a mistake," "make a research," "say an opinion," "take a decision," "do an effort."
2. Check each against the natural English collocation ("make a mistake," "do research," "give/state an opinion," "make a decision," "make an effort").
3. Say each corrected version aloud 3 times, recorded.
Rules:
- Attempt the phrase before checking the correction — this reveals your actual L1-influenced habits.
How to check: Initial attempt and corrected version both recorded for all 5.
drills.check: Initial attempt and corrected version both recorded for all 5.

drills.title: Rapid Pairing Sprint
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Have someone say 10 nouns from your field, one at a time (or record yourself reading them with 4-second gaps).
2. For each noun, say the correct verb collocation within 3 seconds ("decision" → "make a decision").
3. Record the full sprint.
4. Self-mark: Correct / Incorrect per item.
Rules:
- 3-second start rule applies.
How to check: 10 items attempted, each marked correct or incorrect.
drills.check: 10 items attempted, each marked correct or incorrect.

drills.title: Chunk Review Integration
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Confirm all chunks from Drill 17.1 and corrected phrases from Drill 17.2 are entered in the Vocabulary Pipeline with today's date.
2. They now follow the Ch.16 Day 3/7/14/30 review schedule automatically.
Rules:
- No new review system — this uses the Ch.16 pipeline only.
How to check: All new chunks appear in the pipeline table with today's date.
drills.check: All new chunks appear in the pipeline table with today's date.

mistakes: Learning words individually instead of as fixed partnerships.
Translating collocations word-for-word from your native language.
Skipping the "attempt before checking" step in Drill 17.2, which hides your real error patterns.
Adding collocations to a separate list instead of the Ch.16 pipeline.
Choosing collocations you already know well instead of ones you actually needed and lacked.
mission: Use 3 collocations correctly in real speech or writing today. Log which 3 and whether they came out automatically.
checklist: Drill 17.1: 8 chunks extracted, logged, and used in sentences
Drill 17.2: 5 phrases attempted and corrected, both recorded
Drill 17.3: 10-item sprint recorded and self-marked
Drill 17.4: all new chunks confirmed in the pipeline
Real-World Mission done, 3 collocations logged
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: BUILD YOUR CHUNK BANK

drills.title: Functional Chunk Collection
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Collect 10 functional speech chunks across these categories: buying time ("let me think about that," "that's a good question"), hedging ("as far as I know," "I'd say"), signposting ("on the other hand," "the thing is"), and opinion ("from my perspective," "what I'd suggest is").
2. Add all 10 to your Vocabulary Pipeline (Ch.16) as chunk entries.
3. Say each one aloud alone, recorded.
Rules:
- These must be full functional phrases, not single words — distinct from Ch.17's word-pair collocations.
How to check: 10 chunks logged, each recorded alone.
drills.check: 10 chunks logged, each recorded alone.

drills.title: Slot-Filling Practice
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Take 5 of your chunks from Drill 18.1.
2. For each, speak 2 different full sentences using that chunk as the opening frame, with different content plugged in each time.
3. Record all 10 sentences.
Rules:
- The chunk stays fixed; only the content after it changes.
How to check: 10 recorded sentences, 2 per chunk, with different content.
drills.check: 10 recorded sentences, 2 per chunk, with different content.

drills.title: Real-Time Chunk Deployment
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Have someone ask you 6 questions about your opinions or plans (or use an AI voice tool).
2. Before each answer, you must open with one chunk from your bank — assign a different chunk to each question in advance.
3. Answer within 3 seconds of the question ending, starting with the assigned chunk.
4. Record all 6.
Rules:
- 3-second start rule applies.
- No repeating the same chunk twice in this drill.
How to check: 6 recorded answers, each opening with a different assigned chunk.
drills.check: 6 recorded answers, each opening with a different assigned chunk.

drills.title: Pipeline Integration
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Confirm all 10 chunks from Drill 18.1 are in the Vocabulary Pipeline table with today's date.
2. They now follow the Ch.16 Day 3/7/14/30 review schedule.
Rules:
- No separate tracking system.
How to check: All 10 chunks appear in the pipeline table.
drills.check: All 10 chunks appear in the pipeline table.

mistakes: Memorizing chunks silently without producing them in full sentences.
Using the same 1-2 chunks repeatedly instead of rotating through the bank.
Treating chunks as filler instead of using them to buy real processing time before the main content.
Confusing chunks with collocations from Ch.17 — chunks are full functional phrases, collocations are word pairs.
Skipping the 3-second start rule in Drill 18.3 and pre-planning full answers.
mission: In a real conversation today, use 3 chunks from your bank, especially at moments you need a second to think. Log which 3 and whether they helped you keep speaking without a long pause.
checklist: Drill 18.1: 10 chunks logged and recorded
Drill 18.2: 10 sentences recorded across 5 chunks
Drill 18.3: 6 answers recorded with assigned chunks, 3-second rule followed
Drill 18.4: all chunks confirmed in the pipeline
Real-World Mission done, 3 chunks logged
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: USE HIGH-FREQUENCY PHRASAL VERBS

drills.title: Frequency-First Collection
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Collect 10 phrasal verbs you hear often but rarely use yourself, prioritizing everyday and workplace ones: "figure out," "come up with," "run into," "follow up," "point out," "deal with," "carry out," "look into," "set up," "back up."
2. Add all 10 to your Vocabulary Pipeline (Ch.16).
3. Say each one aloud in an original sentence, recorded.
Rules:
- Prioritize the ones you already recognize on hearing but don't produce — not obscure ones.
How to check: 10 phrasal verbs logged, 10 sentences recorded.
drills.check: 10 phrasal verbs logged, 10 sentences recorded.

drills.title: Formal Substitution Drill
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Take 5 formal single-word verbs you already use often ("investigate," "postpone," "resolve," "consider," "encounter").
2. For each, replace it with its phrasal verb equivalent ("look into," "put off," "sort out/deal with," "look into/think over," "run into").
3. Say each sentence twice: once with the formal verb, once with the phrasal verb.
4. Record both versions.
Rules:
- Meaning must stay identical between the two versions.
How to check: 10 recordings (5 pairs), both versions clear.
drills.check: 10 recordings (5 pairs), both versions clear.

drills.title: Separable vs. Inseparable Check
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Take 5 phrasal verbs from your pipeline that take an object ("figure out," "point out," "carry out," "set up," "back up").
2. For each, say one sentence with the object between the verb and particle ("figure it out"), and one with the object after ("figure out the problem").
3. Record all 10.
4. Mark which pattern felt natural and which felt forced.
Rules:
- Use a pronoun object ("it") for the separated version, a full noun for the other.
How to check: 10 recordings, natural/forced marked for each.
drills.check: 10 recordings, natural/forced marked for each.

drills.title: Rapid Recall Sprint
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Have someone give you 8 single-word verb prompts from Drill 19.2's category (investigate, postpone, resolve, consider, encounter, and 3 more of your own).
2. Say the phrasal verb equivalent within 3 seconds, in a full sentence.
3. Record all 8.
4. Self-mark: Correct / Incorrect.
Rules:
- 3-second start rule applies.
How to check: 8 items attempted and marked.
drills.check: 8 items attempted and marked.

mistakes: Collecting rare or overly casual phrasal verbs instead of high-frequency workplace ones.
Only using the formal verb in real speech because it feels safer — the target is natural spoken register.
Never testing separable vs. inseparable patterns, leading to word-order errors under pressure.
Adding phrasal verbs to a separate list instead of the Ch.16 pipeline.
Treating recognition of a phrasal verb while listening as proof you can produce it.
mission: Use 3 phrasal verbs from your bank in real speech or writing today, in place of a formal verb you'd normally default to. Log which 3.
checklist: Drill 19.1: 10 phrasal verbs logged and recorded
Drill 19.2: 5 formal/phrasal pairs recorded
Drill 19.3: separable/inseparable patterns tested and marked
Drill 19.4: 8-item sprint recorded and self-marked
Real-World Mission done, 3 phrasal verbs logged
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: LEARN FEWER IDIOMS, USE THEM RIGHT
intro: CHAPTER 20 — LEARN FEWER IDIOMS, USE THEM RIGHT

drills.title: High-Value Selection
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Select exactly 8 idioms common in professional/technical spoken English: "on the same page," "back to the drawing board," "in the loop," "cut corners," "bottom line," "rule of thumb," "ballpark figure," "touch base."
2. Add all 8 to your Vocabulary Pipeline (Ch.16).
3. Say each one aloud in an original sentence tied to your actual work, recorded.
Rules:
- This list is capped at 8 for this chapter — do not add more idioms this week.
How to check: 8 idioms logged, 8 sentences recorded.
drills.check: 8 idioms logged, 8 sentences recorded.

drills.title: Context-Fit Check
drills.body:
Steps:
1. For each of the 8 idioms, name out loud, recorded, one specific real situation from your work or studies where it would fit naturally.
2. Then name one situation where using it would sound wrong (too casual, wrong audience, wrong moment).
Rules:
- Both a correct-fit and a wrong-fit example are required per idiom.
How to check: 16 recorded examples (8 correct-fit, 8 wrong-fit).
drills.check: 16 recorded examples (8 correct-fit, 8 wrong-fit).

drills.title: Literal vs. Actual Meaning
drills.body:
Steps:
1. For each idiom, say out loud, recorded, what the words would mean literally, then what the idiom actually means.
2. Confirm you can explain the gap between the two for all 8.
Rules:
- Both the literal and actual meaning must be stated for each idiom.
How to check: 8 recorded literal/actual meaning pairs.
drills.check: 8 recorded literal/actual meaning pairs.

drills.title: Restrained Deployment
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Have a 3-minute recorded conversation or monologue on a work topic.
2. Use exactly 1 idiom from your bank, naturally placed, once.
3. Replay and confirm it fit the context and wasn't overused or forced.
Rules:
- Maximum 1 idiom per 3 minutes of speech in this drill — idiom density above this sounds unnatural.
How to check: One recording with exactly 1 natural idiom use, self-confirmed as fitting.
drills.check: One recording with exactly 1 natural idiom use, self-confirmed as fitting.

mistakes: Collecting large idiom lists instead of a small, high-value set.
Using more than 1-2 idioms in a short stretch of speech, which sounds unnatural even to native ears.
Using an idiom without knowing its correct register (too casual for a formal meeting, or the reverse).
Confusing literal meaning with actual meaning and misusing an idiom as a result.
Prioritizing idioms over the higher-value work in Ch.16-19 — idioms are the lowest-priority vocabulary category in this book.
mission: Use exactly 1 idiom from your bank in a real professional conversation today, where it genuinely fits. Log which one and how it landed.
checklist: Drill 20.1: 8 idioms logged and recorded
Drill 20.2: 16 fit/wrong-fit examples recorded
Drill 20.3: 8 literal/actual meaning pairs recorded
Drill 20.4: 3-minute recording with exactly 1 natural idiom use
Real-World Mission done, outcome logged
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: AUDIT THE CORE 2,000 WORDS

drills.title: Core Batch Audit
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Get a batch of 25 common English words ranked roughly 1-2000 by frequency (ask an AI tool for "25 common English words from frequency rank [pick a 100-word range you haven't tested], excluding technical terms," rotating the range each session).
2. For each word, attempt to say it aloud in an original sentence within 3 seconds of seeing it.
3. Record the full batch.
4. Mark each: Active (produced smoothly) / Gap (hesitated or couldn't produce a natural sentence).
Rules:
- Pick everyday words, not technical vocabulary — the core list is general-purpose (e.g., "arrange," "roughly," "afford," "eventually").
How to check: 25 words attempted, each marked Active or Gap.
drills.check: 25 words attempted, each marked Active or Gap.

drills.title: Gap Closure Sprint
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Take every word marked Gap from Drill 21.1.
2. Add all of them to your Vocabulary Pipeline (Ch.16), today's date.
3. Say each one aloud in 2 different original sentences, recorded.
Rules:
- These go through the normal Ch.16 spaced review schedule — no shortcuts.
How to check: All Gap words logged in the pipeline, 2 sentences each recorded.
drills.check: All Gap words logged in the pipeline, 2 sentences each recorded.

drills.title: Coverage Estimate Log
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Calculate this session's Active percentage (Active count ÷ 25).
2. Log it with today's date in a running Core Coverage table in Appendix E.
3. After 4+ sessions, compare percentages across dates to track trend.
Rules:
- Use the same 25-word batch size every session for comparable percentages.
How to check: A dated coverage percentage logged, trend visible after multiple sessions.
drills.check: A dated coverage percentage logged, trend visible after multiple sessions.

drills.title: Rotating Sample Schedule
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Schedule one Core Batch Audit (Drill 21.1) per week until you've sampled at least 300 words total.
2. Write the next session's date and frequency range into your practice log now.
Rules:
- Each week's batch must come from a different frequency range than previous weeks.
How to check: Next session scheduled with a specific new frequency range noted.
drills.check: Next session scheduled with a specific new frequency range noted.

mistakes: Testing only technical or academic vocabulary and assuming it represents the general-purpose core.
Assuming a word is Active because you understand it while reading — this drill tests spoken production only.
Skipping Drill 21.2 and leaving Gap words untracked instead of feeding them into the pipeline.
Testing the same frequency range repeatedly instead of rotating through new ranges.
Treating one session's high coverage percentage as proof the whole core is covered.
mission: Use 3 words that were marked Gap in a past session, now graduated or in review, in a real conversation today. Log which 3.
checklist: Drill 21.1: 25-word batch audited and marked
Drill 21.2: all Gap words logged in the pipeline with sentences
Drill 21.3: coverage percentage logged
Drill 21.4: next session scheduled with a new frequency range
Real-World Mission done, 3 words logged
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: SPEAK YOUR TECHNICAL VOCABULARY

drills.title: Glue Word Audit
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Take these 10 semi-technical glue words used across engineering fields: "parameter," "correlate," "derive," "approximate," "constraint," "optimize," "significant," "variable," "methodology," "iterate."
2. For each, say aloud whether you use it in writing but not speech, or neither.
3. Add every word you don't currently speak fluently to your Vocabulary Pipeline (Ch.16).
4. Say each one aloud in a sentence about your actual work, recorded.
Rules:
- These are cross-field glue words, not narrow specialty terms — confirm you know all 10 before moving on.
How to check: 10 words assessed, gaps logged in the pipeline, sentences recorded.
drills.check: 10 words assessed, gaps logged in the pipeline, sentences recorded.

drills.title: Field Term Speaking Drill
drills.body:
Steps:
1. List 8 technical terms specific to your field that you read/write comfortably but rarely say out loud.
2. For each, speak one full sentence explaining what it means, as if to a colleague who half-knows the topic.
3. Record all 8.
4. Replay and mark: Explained clearly / Explanation broke down.
Rules:
- Explanation must define or use the term functionally, not just name it.
How to check: 8 recorded explanations, each marked.
drills.check: 8 recorded explanations, each marked.

drills.title: Describe a Graph or Diagram
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Pick one graph, chart, or diagram from your coursework or a recent project.
2. Describe it out loud for 90 seconds: what it shows, the trend or structure, and one conclusion you'd draw from it.
3. Record this.
4. Replay and mark any point where you paused searching for a word.
Rules:
- No reading from notes during the description.
How to check: One 90-second recorded description with pause points marked.
drills.check: One 90-second recorded description with pause points marked.

drills.title: Timed Technical Explanation Sprint
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Pick one concept from this week's coursework.
2. Set a 2-minute timer. Explain it out loud to a non-expert, using at least 5 terms from Drills 22.1-22.2.
3. Record the full explanation.
4. Replay and count how many of the 5 target terms you actually used.
Rules:
- Explanation must stay understandable to someone outside your field.
How to check: A 2-minute recording with a confirmed count of target terms used (5 or more required).
drills.check: A 2-minute recording with a confirmed count of target terms used (5 or more required).

mistakes: Assuming a technical term is "known" because you can read and write it fluently.
Practicing only narrow specialty terms and skipping the cross-field glue words in Drill 22.1.
Reading from notes during the graph description instead of speaking freely.
Using overly complex terms in Drill 22.4 that confuse a non-expert instead of explaining clearly.
Treating this chapter as separate from Ch.16-21 — all new terms route through the same pipeline.
mission: Explain one technical concept from today's studies out loud to a real person (or recorded, addressed to a camera) for 2 minutes, using at least 5 target terms from this chapter.
checklist: Drill 22.1: 10 glue words assessed, gaps logged, sentences recorded
Drill 22.2: 8 field terms explained and recorded
Drill 22.3: graph/diagram description recorded, pauses marked
Drill 22.4: 2-minute technical explanation recorded, 5+ terms confirmed
Real-World Mission done, term count confirmed
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: BUILD RAW SPEAKING STAMINA

drills.title: The 4/3/2 Technique
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Pick one topic ("a project I'm working on").
2. Speak about it for 4 minutes straight, recorded, no stopping.
3. Immediately speak about the exact same topic again for 3 minutes, recorded — same content, compressed.
4. Immediately speak about the same topic again for 2 minutes, recorded — same content, compressed further.
Rules:
- Content stays the same across all three rounds; only time and pace change.
- No stopping mid-round for any reason.
How to check: Three recordings of decreasing length on identical content, each fully continuous.
drills.check: Three recordings of decreasing length on identical content, each fully continuous.

drills.title: Timed Speaking Ladder
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Get 3 random topics you haven't prepared (ask someone, or use an AI tool to generate random topics).
2. Speak on topic 1 for 30 seconds, recorded.
3. Speak on topic 2 for 60 seconds, recorded.
4. Speak on topic 3 for 90 seconds, recorded.
Rules:
- No preparation time before each topic — start within 5 seconds of receiving it.
How to check: Three recordings at 30/60/90 seconds, each starting within 5 seconds of the prompt.
drills.check: Three recordings at 30/60/90 seconds, each starting within 5 seconds of the prompt.

drills.title: Rapid Q&A Chain
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Have someone (or an AI voice tool) ask you 10 questions back to back, with no more than 5 seconds between each.
2. Answer every question within 3 seconds of it ending.
3. Record the full chain.
4. Mark any question where you broke the 3-second rule.
Rules:
- Questions must come rapid-fire — no long gaps between them.
How to check: 10 answers recorded, broken-rule questions marked.
drills.check: 10 answers recorded, broken-rule questions marked.

drills.title: Filler Replacement Drill
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Speak on a topic for 2 minutes, recorded.
2. Every time you hesitate, use an English chunk from your Ch.18 bank ("let me think about that," "what I mean is") instead of silence or a native-language filler.
3. Replay and count: chunk uses vs. silent pauses vs. native-language fillers.
Rules:
- No native-language filler words allowed during this drill.
How to check: A count of chunk uses vs. remaining silent/native fillers, chunk use dominant.
drills.check: A count of chunk uses vs. remaining silent/native fillers, chunk use dominant.

mistakes: Stopping mid-recording in the 4/3/2 technique to plan the next sentence.
Using preparation time before the Timed Speaking Ladder topics.
Letting silence stretch past 3 seconds during the Rapid Q&A Chain.
Falling back on native-language filler words instead of the English chunks from Ch.18.
Choosing easy, familiar topics instead of random ones that force real-time production.
mission: Ask a real person to give you a random topic. Speak on it, unprepared, for 3 minutes. Record if possible. Note how many times you used a chunk instead of a filler or silence.
checklist: Drill 23.1: three 4/3/2 recordings completed
Drill 23.2: three ladder recordings at 30/60/90 seconds
Drill 23.3: 10-question rapid chain recorded, rule breaks marked
Drill 23.4: 2-minute recording with chunk/filler count
Real-World Mission done, chunk use noted
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: HANDLE A REAL CONVERSATION

drills.title: Clarification Request Bank
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Learn these 5 phrases: "Sorry, could you say that again?", "What do you mean by that?", "Could you explain that a bit more?", "Sorry, I didn't catch that.", "Do you mean [X]?"
2. Add all 5 to your Vocabulary Pipeline (Ch.16).
3. Say each aloud 3 times, recorded, in a natural tone.
Rules:
- Tone must sound like a real request, not a memorized line — vary stress and pace slightly each time.
How to check: 5 phrases, 3 recordings each, natural delivery confirmed on replay.
drills.check: 5 phrases, 3 recordings each, natural delivery confirmed on replay.

drills.title: Conversation Repair Practice
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Explain a technical idea to a real person or an AI voice tool.
2. When they show confusion or ask you to repeat, do not repeat identically — rephrase the idea a different way.
3. Record the exchange if possible.
4. Afterward, log: what confused them, and how you rephrased it.
Rules:
- Rephrasing must use different words or structure, not a slower repeat of the same sentence.
How to check: A logged example of one rephrase attempt, different from the original wording.
drills.check: A logged example of one rephrase attempt, different from the original wording.

drills.title: Turn-Taking Signals
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Learn these signals: "Can I add something?", "Sorry to jump in, but—", "Go ahead.", "That's a good point, and also—"
2. In a real or simulated conversation with 2+ exchanges, use at least 2 of these signals naturally.
3. Record the conversation if possible.
4. Log which signals you used and where.
Rules:
- Signals must be used in a live back-and-forth, not read aloud in isolation.
How to check: A logged conversation with 2+ turn-taking signals used in context.
drills.check: A logged conversation with 2+ turn-taking signals used in context.

drills.title: Keep-It-Alive Drill
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Have someone ask you 5 questions.
2. Answer each, then add one follow-up question of your own before they speak again.
3. Record all 5 exchanges.
Rules:
- Every answer must end with a follow-up question — no answer-only turns in this drill.
How to check: 5 recorded exchanges, each including a follow-up question.
drills.check: 5 recorded exchanges, each including a follow-up question.

mistakes: Staying silent or nodding when you don't understand instead of using a clarification phrase.
Repeating the exact same sentence, louder or slower, when asked to clarify instead of rephrasing.
Avoiding interruption entirely, letting the conversation stall instead of using a turn-taking signal.
Giving short, closed answers that end the conversation instead of adding a follow-up question.
Treating these phrases as things to recognize, not things to actively practice saying.
mission: In one real conversation today, deliberately use one clarification or repair phrase and one follow-up question. Log both moments and how the conversation responded.
checklist: Drill 24.1: 5 phrases recorded 3x each
Drill 24.2: one rephrase example logged
Drill 24.3: 2+ turn-taking signals used and logged
Drill 24.4: 5 exchanges recorded with follow-up questions
Real-World Mission done, both moments logged
All items logged in practice log (Appendix E)

title: TELL A STRUCTURED STORY

drills.title: Narrative Skeleton Drill
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Pick a real event from your life (a problem you solved, a trip, a mistake).
2. Tell it in 3 parts, out loud, recorded: Setup (situation), Complication (what went wrong or changed), Resolution (how it ended).
3. Say "Setup," "Complication," "Resolution" out loud as you transition between parts, then redo the story without saying the labels.
Rules:
- All 3 parts must be present and in order.
How to check: Two recordings — one with spoken labels, one without — both containing all 3 parts.
drills.check: Two recordings — one with spoken labels, one without — both containing all 3 parts.

drills.title: Timeline Connectors Drill
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Retell the same story from Drill 25.1.
2. Use at least 5 different timeline connectors: "first," "then," "after that," "eventually," "in the end," "meanwhile," "as soon as."
3. Record it.
4. Replay and mark which connectors you used.
Rules:
- No repeating the same connector more than twice.
How to check: 5+ distinct connectors identified on replay.
drills.check: 5+ distinct connectors identified on replay.

drills.title: Detail Layering
drills.body:
Steps:
1. Tell a short story (60 seconds) with minimal detail, recorded.
2. Tell the same story again, 90 seconds, adding specific sensory or concrete details (what you saw, heard, exact numbers, exact words someone said).
3. Compare both recordings.
Rules:
- Second version must add real detail, not filler words.
How to check: Two recordings, second one longer and more specific.
drills.check: Two recordings, second one longer and more specific.

drills.title: Cold Story Prompt
drills.body:
Steps:
